title: 2026 02 11 FEC
displayTitle: 2026-02-11 - Finance and Expenditure Committee - Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
topic: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition
description: Wednesday 11 February 2026| Finance and Expenditure Committee Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition Bank of New Zealand Warwick Hunt, Chair Dan Huggins, Chief Executive
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the response to the inquiry into banking competition. Bank of New Zealand. Warwick Hunt, Chair

chunks.text: And non bank deposit takers and fintechs over the next couple of weeks. I just remind everyone, this is a parliament. This is part of a parliamentary inquiry, so I'm going to go from side to side of the table. And so I encourage you to kind of ask questions in a more in a strategic fashion, and I encourage you to use the basis of your questioning from the written responses that the BNZ and others have submitted a week ahead of their appearances. So welcome to the chair and to the chief executive. We look forward to some brief introductory remarks, and then we'll get started.

Thank you. Thank you. Well, thanks for having us and Warwick, I'll hand you Kia ora, Kanata, kau Tou, good morning, everybody. Thank you very much indeed for inviting us to meet with you. Clearly, we have responded to your questions in writing, and we look forward to hearing further commentary and questions around that. Beyond that, I understand we have relatively limited time, so I wasn't going to take up any more time with the committee and simply invite your questions to Dan or myself or both.

Okay, thank you. That was that was right ahead of time. Okay, now I'm going to go to orina Kia, ora Kota. Thank you so much for joining us to bring back, obviously, really interested in some of these topics that you've answered us about, and thank you for your openness about that. I want to ask you is, are the major banks locking in their market power now, in this period that we're in, in the development of fintechs, are you seeing that major banks are behaving in New Zealand market together in a way that hinders the development of smaller, innovative firms trying to compete with you?

That's not what we're seeing. I mean, obviously the open banking standards all went live in December, and we met those, those commitments for December. We've now got 35 fintechs that are onboarded to use those APIs with open banking. We've got another 34 fintechs that are in those sort of sandbox, those test environments, or in the process of being onboarding onboarded. About 300,000 customers are accessing those APIs and using it. So yeah, our viewers, and in fact, if you look globally, they're pretty good, like New Zealand is experienced with open banking has been pretty good relative to what we've seen in other jurisdictions. So yeah, I think what we're seeing is quite good competition coming through the fintechs alive and well and being able to compete pretty aggressively. The

government's proposed some new powers for the Commerce Commission to look at larger firms acquiring smaller firms in a competitive environment. Should the Commerce Commission be worried about, say, BNZ, because blink pay, or other banks acquiring smaller fintechs who compete with them? Yeah. Look, I think it's important that the Commerce Commission has the powers to look at these things and make sure they can satisfy themselves that there isn't a reduction in competition. When we look at blink pay, or central pay, the two fintechs that that we acquired recently, we've invested more money in those two businesses than they've invested for their entire life. So I think there's also significant benefits. And I think Adrian from blink pay is here late this afternoon, so I think you can direct your questions to him as well directly. But our view is that there's a strength that we can bring to some of those institutions to allow them to accelerate innovation and then now putting products into market that are available for everyone, all banks, not just the B NZ. So if anything, it's strengthening competition from our

perspective. Just a final one, just picking up on what you said, which I think is true. You know, open banking seems to be going relatively well when we compare ourselves to overseas jurisdictions who have had a much slower uptake. Yeah. Could New Zealand be a leader in the uptake of open banking services, and what would that require for the major banks to adopt some of those things that would see a faster uptake.

Yeah, I think we were already there, actually. I think, you know, when you look at the adoption of open banking relative to others, UK or Australia, actually, if anything, New Zealand is leading. I think, as we discussed last time we were here, what's, what's key to continued uptake is trust with custom customers and making sure that they see this as trusted, that therefore they're happy to use it. So I think as long as that continues, we've got now an open framework people can onboard and can get accredited to utilise that data. I think that's positive, and uptake is now going to all be about innovative solutions on that that customers trust and use. But my people, Ori anything from you

look, I agree with that entirely. I think if you look at the rate of uptake in New Zealand, it's encouraging. That's ahead of us now, and that's up to all these participants, the regulators ourselves, as the major banking institutions and clearly the fintechs, who are actually bringing much of the innovation, but not all the innovation. I mean, a lot of that comes from our sector too.

Okay, we're going to go to Dan bidwar Thank you, Mr. Chair. Welcome back Dan and Warwick. Thanks for your time. Today, I want to talk about BNS net interest margin, because we've previously heard from you that the interest margin in New Zealand is higher than Australia. In fact. You gave three main areas on why that's the case. One of those is around the capital requirements. And noticed in your submission today, you didn't touch on the impact of the capital requirements. So I'm keen to get your sense of what's Carmel reserve bank in the last few months, no changes in what that will likely mean for your net interest margin in New Zealand, New Zealand?

Yeah, absolutely. So look, I think we should start by saying that the review, which I'm certainly encouraged by this committee, that the reserve bank took on on the capital, has resulted in positive changes. You know, we will see lower capital being required between sort of now versus where we would have been in 2028 and that's going to result in lower cost of credit for Kiwis, you know, kiwi households and businesses. I think we can clearly see that. So that's really positive. And, you know, is a good outcome. You know, if we look at the magnitude of those changes versus where we sort of, some of the changes that have been made are relative to where we would have been in 2028 so you're talking about, well, this is how much capital you would have had to have held versus what's the case now and but when we look at it, you know, we would say that interest rates overall be about 20 basis points lower than they would have been in 2028 and that varies pretty significantly by different sectors. So you know, if you're a low LV R agri customer, and you were previously being assessed on standardised now you're on advance. You know, it's more like 50 to 75 basis points for an average mortgage holder, more like 15. So, you know, we those. Those are pretty real impacts, and that's going to be very positive for New Zealand. You know, we expect that in this competitive environment, those, those will be, those will be passed on to customers, you know, through 2028 and therefore you're not going to see, you know that that, you know, captured a nim. Nim will move around a little bit, you know, as we see, different funding costs, and we've chatted about what, you know, some of the drivers of those things, but our expectation is that's going to be handed off to customers. And 20, you know, by 2028 Thank

you. That 20 basis points is certainly positive. It is lower than the estimate that you provided us. You know, around the likely quantum of the impact for business lending and equity lending, yeah. Why is that? Because RB, NZ could have gone further, or when we when we last spoke, we talked about versus 2018 to 2028 what was the total impact? And what you've seen the changes do is not take you all the way back to 2018 levels. It's, you know, roughly halfway. So I think the numbers we're talking about here are about half of that, of that potential difference that we talked about with to 2018 so material, but sort of we haven't gone all the way back to the 2018 levels. There will still be more capital held in the system versus where we were in 2018

Okay. Thank you very much. But having said that, and sorry, just just adding to that, I think what is important here is that the capital changes, you know that have actually been proposed walk that fine line pretty successfully between efficiency and also maintaining the Prudential sum of solar system. Great. Thank you. Okay, gonna go back on this side. Thank you. I want to ask you about interchange fees and merchant fees. BNZ was one of the first to launch a product pay app that is designed to circumvent surcharging from merchants. Retailers were sort of part of the bad cop in that way that it was advertised, where retailers were portrayed as adding on charges to their customers over and above the cost of doing the transactions. How is it? How is this going to roll out in New Zealand's market, where retailers are already feeling the pinch and their fees are increasingly lacking in transparency around merchant fees and interchange fees, but we're also now seeing from the banks more aggressive rhetoric around those retailers being the issue.

Yeah. I mean, I think pay app is a good example of the way that open banking helps create new payments, cheaper ways of paying, which will help consumers and retailers. So with pay app, you know, at a point of sale, it's about 39 basis points that someone's going to pay, versus multitudes of that through other payment mechanisms, still simple and easy, uses a QR code, so pretty straightforward for for a customer. You know, the savings online for people are higher. So it's 59 basis points for an online payment. You know, when someone's using other methods of payment. Now, people can be paying 200 300 400 basis points for that. So, you know, we think it's a good example of open banking coming in, providing cheaper ways of paying, and that's that's going to help merchants so that they can use those services and save what they otherwise would have been

paying. I agree. I think the commies commission couple of years ago now, was indicating to the major players in the industry that about, you know, about 1% would be the fees that they would expect a competitive market to be charging. New Zealanders are mostly paying much. Much higher than that at the point of sale, and a large proportion of that is is at the banking stage. And do you think that New Zealand's market moving in the right direction around the fees we are paying for transactions, and is it fair for the retailers who are also then not able to pass on those costs in some Yeah, I

think the the Commerce Commission of obvious has obviously changed those interchange fees, and that's been reflected. It's been a first round of those that have been reflected now, and all of those fees have been have been lowered. There's another round of that coming for international cards. So, you know, we've definitely seen those fees come down in line with with the commerce commissions, you know, requirements, and we've transferred those on, you know, in terms of the, you know, we heard a lot from customers about them not liking surcharge, and, you know, it feels like another fee, and it just, you don't really, you don't really like, know, the cost. So, you know, we think that it is good for Kiwis to have one, one price, and then, you know, it's on us to make sure we're offering for merchants cheaper ways to pay, you know, which is obviously what we're trying to do with pay app. So, you know, I think it's a balance on both. It's a balance on both sides of keeping customers happy, keeping Kiwis happy so they know what they're paying, while at the same time providing payment services that are that are cheaper for

merchants. You're right. Interchange fees are now regulated in New Zealand, the merchant fees are largely unregulated and largely untransparent. If you're the cookie merchant in Hamilton, on the main street, you can't tell what BNZ might be charging you in any given month for the number of transactions. Should that be transparent? We are transparent with those merchants about what that what they're paying. So that is that is very transparently, certainly from the BNZ side, as to exactly what the fees are and how that breaks

up. Should it be clearer in any given month then what, what New Zealanders might expect to be paying if they are a small business owner. Is that transparent enough? Or do you think the interchange fees are the thing that will really see some juice there? I think we've seen the exchange fees come down. We're super transparent about those. There are certainly other fees that merchants are wearing, not from the banking sector or other payment methods, you know, depending on what they've done online, or other things that come in and and I think transparency is good. So yeah, certainly, if there are ideas around, hey, here's how you could improve transparency that would, you know, help merchants then, then, yeah, we're certainly, certainly keen to hear that and happy to engage in that conversation.

Okay, thank you. We're gonna go to Todd Stevenson. Thank you. Thank you for being here. I've downloaded Papa. How many of you use it yet? You need some more retailers around parliament. There's 2000 Yeah, sorry, but I'm very keen to try it out. Just what? A couple of questions. Few questions here. Great. And you've talked about, you've got onboarded 35 fintechs. You've got 34 more testing underway with people that are on the 34 and testing like, how quickly can they will move through that process? I'm just trying to understand, like, how quickly can you onboard people and get them into the

I mean, people have to be accredited, obviously, the standards, as long as they're credited. Relatively quick the 34 There's number of people that are using our like, sandboxes, which is our test environments, to see whether the whether they can actually get their products to, you know, to work, and whether it's and then, and then they may move to onboarding. So in that 34 there's a number that may say, actually, I don't need to be on board. There's going through that get on board because it's relatively just to get people, like, talking weeks months. We're not talking years,

though, right? It's not years, and it's even months. So I think it's weeks, but someone's, you know, accredited in that, in that way, it's relatively there's some technical things that people are needing to do, so like, yeah, depends on whether someone's got the right tech to connect up with the API's, but if someone's got all of that lined up, it's, you know, I suppose, just getting clarity, not creating any unnecessary barriers to try and move people through that? No, it's pretty straightforward from you. So thanks and great, and thanks for your submission. I particularly like how you've gone through the recommendations and addressed them all. I want to push you on one of them, which is around recommendation 11, around payments New Zealand. I mean, you Benz as a shareholder of payments New Zealand. And yeah, and great. You support the obviously, as a committee, we made some recommendations around governance, and basically, just the broad point we're making is that needs to be opened up. We appreciate, obviously, some of the banks are shareholders, but that that organisation needs to be opened up. You've said you're supportive of that governance review, but can you actively, what are you doing to ensure that happens?

Look and you're getting the sense of the answer of with a sort of fine line of what payment is an independent board. It's an independent entity. And yes, we are a shareholder and a participant, but we need to respect their independence. But certainly we hear you on, you know, there's an opportunity here to improve the transparency, the competition, to open it up. Obviously, that's a decision for yes, for them independently, but, you know, we'll engage with them in that and we understand that the committee's recommendations in that space, and you know, we'll, we'll work with payments New Zealand to realise that, I suppose.

How do I put this? But as a shareholder, is your expectation that should be done, you know, by the end of this year, within 12 months? I mean, are you able to, kind of, just, I. I know I look because, yeah, I mean, I honestly, I don't know the answer to that is, throughout quickly the board is going to be able to do those things, certainly from a shareholder perspective, our expectation is that, you know, we're meeting the expectations of you as the committee and the New Zealand public. So, you know, we'll be working with payments New Zealand to make sure that those things are realised in whatever form that takes and and then as quickly as possible. From their perspective, this is kind of my broad expectation, and that's not a straightforward answer to your question, but that's kind of my expectation. It's just an error we do want to keep because I think it could actually be beneficial to everyone. Okay, thank you.

Just to follow up on that. Ah, sorry. Oh no, okay, let's go to Rawiri. Surprise, surprise. My mine will my questions will be around recommendation 16 and 19, sure. And I know we're part of the conversations around what you did with Ngati, whatua, we know recently they've just not those funny. I want to know whether these are examples out there where you've had the same impact on other communities when it comes to freehold Maori lane, and if there's been any substantial growth in that particular space, and if not, what is what has been done about it to ensure that might have access to capital to be able to achieve those goals?

Yeah, it's a fantastic question. And you know, we continue to have a dedicated Maori banking team that's sort of working every day with iwi around the country in different means. What? Right now, there's $189 million lent against Maori freehold land since we, since we put those in place in 23 and then obviously extended in 24 in an ability to enable that Maori freehold land lending. So that's good. We'd love to do more. It's proving pretty tricky, like it's it's hard to do, and we've been talking with a lot of iwi about, whole lot about how we might do that, but it's pretty time consuming, and which is fine, like we were really committed to it. We'd love to do more. At the moment, it's about 190 million the year. There's another 490 odd million, nearly $500 million from from Maori agribusiness perspective. So that's been much bigger. Yeah, you know. But again, we're really committed to it. It's taking longer than we'd like.

Yeah, there's the steps to get the house on Maori free or land is 20 steps, yeah, and then for somebody to build on General Land is about three or four steps, yeah, that's right. And so it's very difficult, absolutely. And obviously it's, it's then looking at, you know, working with the land trusts and the incorporations, and making sure that that the entire iwi is comfortable with what's being proposed those they necessarily take time. So we are very aware that that takes time, and that consultation, that getting that comfort, is really important. And I think one thing we are very cognizant of is we need to actually make the bank work with Maori, and it needs to actually fit in that world, rather than in our world. So that's what we're trying to do, and and respect that. Well, these things have a process and take time. I'd love for them to go faster. I'd love to put more capital work there, but we're pushing but then respecting that, that these things are going to take time and development

in that particular Okay, thank you. Ryan, thanks. Chair. Dan, just a couple of questions around, I guess, payments New Zealand, and following on from Todd, and you talk about supporting the question that came out in the inquiry around payments New Zealand, is it a little bit like checking your own homework, though, in terms of, like, want to prove the governance structure, but you are obviously BNZ is 19% shareholder, and I guess to ask a further question and blink pay in their submission and respect that you guys have a professional relationship, he's made some suggestions around how we could tighten up that governance structure. So I just mentioned your thoughts on making it more independent.

Yeah, I think when payments New Zealand was was formed that had quite a tight, you know, kind of mandate, it's pretty clear that mandates, you know, expanding, particularly with open banking, and therefore there are going to be changes that are needed, whether, whether you can keep holding some of that stuff in payments New Zealand, or whether it actually needs to come out into a different entity that that's, you know, independent in its own right and has more participants. I think all those topics are up for conversation. But I think your observations right around, you know, there's a there can be that perception accurately of, well, hang on, you've got the banks, you know, in this entity that's looking after things that that, in some cases, you know, the banks, you know, maybe, maybe are less, are less comfortable with so, you know, I think those conversations are having other right conversations. I think that perception is, you know, is one that we just need to reflect, and whatever changes are made through that and and from blink pay and Adrian, yeah, look, I mean, I, you know, blink pay runs independent. Confidently. They've got an independent view on things. They'll come here and talk to that clearly. You know, sometimes those views are different from ours, and that's okay they have and

just Just quickly on that. He also talks about version 3.0 in terms of clarity for the market. Can payments New Zealand provide more clarity and milestones? Because those that, I guess, ambiguity is creating some challenge for them. Yeah, this is the one for me to take back to payment New Zealand and make sure they provide that. I've certainly seen that from Adrian as well, and in my chats with the team about three point I thought it was a little clearer. It was clearer to us around Hey, when do we need to deliver this by? And what are those dates? But it sounds like that's been less transparent in the market. So that's a good one for us take back to payments New Zealand and make sure that's sorted out.

Thank you. Okay. Alina Williams, I just want to ask you about your year end results, because you have some new data around what's going on with the lending book. Can you just give us an insight into, I think, first time buyers, you said last time made up about a third of your lending book. So interested in whether that has changed and the growth in the home loans that you're lending on. Are people borrowing more?

Yeah. So on the first time buyers kind of two answers to that question. The book's so big that, like, we don't see massive movements, kind of in six months to six month periods in the overall portfolio. But what we have seen in the past quarter is that first time buyers have been way more active in the market, and more of the flow that we're seeing is with first home buyers versus what we've seen in previous years, which is good. So you are seeing more first time buyers active in the market. On the total loan value, we haven't seen a huge shift in the total amount of money on average that that people are borrowing, LV, RS have moved around a little bit, but we haven't sort of seen a massive move in the total amount.

Why is it that, with the economy still performing poorly overall, when New Zealand's economy, relative to Australia, not performing but BNS profitability here is more profitable than its Australian counterparts, the profitability of BNS lower than Australia, just when we look at our return equity, we're about the data last report. We're about report, we're about 11% Australia is 11.4 so actually, the New Zealand business is less profitable than Australia. Okay, okay, just, just one from me. You've got here in December the those, the volume of the volume of lending that was moving between banks is over three times the volume than your, your the switching, the previous switching record in July 2024 just, just, what are some of the key factors that that drove that?

Yes, it's a great it's a big question. That's a great question, and one we've been talking about as well, because when we look at what we've done to the switching service, we've added some people, we've improved the SLAs. But I mean, as much as I'd like to take credit for that, probably less less to do with that. I think part of this work, part of the work of the committee, I think, is has raised the profile that switching is actually relatively easy. You know, we spoke about that. I think the perception previously has been that's very hard to do, and that perception feels like it started to shift. Clearly, we've done some advertising, but again, media are focused on those things. It's been talked about a lot. And as a consequence, I think we've seen customers who are really asking that question of, well, can I get a better deal somewhere else, and switching isn't as hard as perhaps I thought, or I can multi bank, as we're seeing quite a lot of where actually, I can move my mortgage to a bank, and I don't have to move all of my transactional banking. So yeah, that's increased the competition, which has been pretty brutal, and in December is the numbers you're talking about. You know, we saw $5.6 billion worth of mortgages move in December, that is a record high where the previous record was 2.6 billion. So it's more than twice the previous record month, which which is good. So I think there's a number of dynamics there. It's very competitive. There were very good offers in market. I think the perception of customers has shifted. I think the work of this committee, others and media has helped ask people you know to challenge that, and we've seen the results.

And you you anticipate that the switching volume will continue, there'll be the record will continue to be broken every every year. We're definitely seeing the switching hold up, and we're seeing, you know, the number of customers that are joining the BNZ now is much, much higher than it was before, so we've definitely seen that persist. I'm not sure that we're going to keep on doing $5.6 billion months, but certainly the aggregated switchings coming up. But one of you might have a view there of what you expect to see.

Yeah, look clearly 5.6 was very, very high and coincided with a trough in interest rates at that particular point in time, activity from some very major players. But the kind of point here, I wanted to make this philosophical, and certainly the board and back end and the executive team here is we absolutely will invest in architecture. Acid to switch, because we back ourselves in a competitive market to actually provide a superior service to our customers. Okay, at least philosophically, that's where we want to meet. Good

way to good way to leave. Hey, so thank you. Thank you to the B and C, the CE and to the chair. And can I also congratulate the comms team on interpreting the brief perfectly succinctly responding to our recommendations as they pertain to the B and C. So think that's going to cause me all sorts of problems with the comm team.
Thank you very much. Appreciate it. Okay, members, we'll just move into private session and.
